text_blocks.text: In the August 1917 edition of the magazine Electrical
Experimenter Tesla postulated that electricity could be used to
locate submarines via using the reflection of an "electric ray"
of "tremendous frequency," with the signal being viewed on a
fluorescent screen (a system that has been noted to have a
superficial resemblance to modern radar). Tesla was incorrect in
his assumption that high frequency radio waves would penetrate
water but Emile Girardeau, who helped develop France's first
radar system in the 1930s, noted in 1953 that Tesla's
general speculation that a very strong high frequency signal
would be needed was correct stating "(Tesla) was prophesying
or dreaming, since he had at his disposal no means of carrying
them out, but one must add that if he was dreaming, at least
